title: OR-Tools CP-SAT FFI integration
analysis:
Task 77 calls for a logical/discrete constraint solver to handle boolean SAT, enum constraints, implications, cardinality, all-different, and integer constraints. The task suggests OR-Tools CP-SAT via FFI but explicitly allows simpler alternatives for v0.1.

After analyzing the codebase, the best v0.1 approach is a pure-Rust backtracking CP solver using the existing expression evaluator (reify_expr::eval_expr) as the constraint checker. This avoids C++ FFI complexity, keeps compile times fast, requires zero new external dependencies, and is easily testable.

The existing infrastructure is perfectly set up for this:
- ConstraintSolver trait in reify-types defines the interface (solve(&self, problem: &ResolutionProblem) -> SolveResult)
- SolverRegistry already has a `logical: Option<Box<dyn ConstraintSolver>>` slot
- ConstraintClassifier already classifies bool-only expressions as ConstraintDomain::Logical
- decompose_into_components routes logical sub-problems to the logical solver
- reify_expr::eval_expr can evaluate any CompiledExpr tree against a ValueMap, serving as the universal constraint checker

The solver algorithm: forward-checking backtracking search over discrete variable domains. For each auto param, the domain is determined by its type (Bool -> {true,false}, Int -> {lo..hi} from bounds, Enum -> variants extracted from constraint literals). Constraints are checked incrementally after each variable assignment using eval_expr, pruning early on violation. This is O(product of domain sizes) in the worst case but with early pruning is very efficient for the small problem sizes expected in v0.1 CAD constraint solving (typically 1-10 discrete variables).

The solver will be named CpSatSolver to match the task spec, implemented in a new cpsat.rs module, exported from lib.rs, and registered as the logical solver in SolverRegistry.

--- Post-review amendment ---
Review identified a blocking integer overflow bug in build_variable_domain (cpsat.rs:55-56): the expression `hi_i - lo_i + 1` overflows in debug builds before the size guard can catch it. The f64-to-i64 saturating casts convert infinity/large f64 values to i64::MAX/MIN, and their difference exceeds i64::MAX. Steps 19-20 add TDD coverage and fix for this.

prerequisites.description: Create cpsat.rs module file with a placeholder CpSatSolver struct and add `mod cpsat` to lib.rs with `pub use cpsat::CpSatSolver`. Verify the crate compiles. No trait impl yet - just the struct definition.

steps.description: Write failing test in cpsat_tests.rs: boolean SAT with 3 Bool auto params (a, b, c) and constraint `a && (b || !c)`. Use value_ref_typed with Type::Bool for param refs, literal(Value::Bool(...)) for constants, and/or/not builders. Create ResolutionProblem with auto_params typed as Type::Bool (no bounds needed). Expect SolveResult::Solved with assignment where a=true and (b=true or c=false).

Implement CpSatSolver core in cpsat.rs: (1) Variable struct with id: ValueCellId, domain: Vec<Value>; (2) build_variable_domains() extracts Bool domains from AutoParam with Type::Bool -> vec![Value::Bool(true), Value::Bool(false)]; (3) collect_constraint_refs() maps each constraint to the auto param ValueCellIds it references using CompiledExpr::walk; (4) backtrack() recursive function: picks next unassigned variable, tries each domain value, inserts into trial ValueMap (cloned from current_values), evaluates all constraints whose variables are fully assigned via reify_expr::eval_expr, prunes on Value::Bool(false), recurses; (5) solve() builds domains, runs backtrack, returns Solved with HashMap<ValueCellId, Value> or Infeasible with diagnostic. Implement ConstraintSolver trait. Export from lib.rs.
Write failing test: infeasible boolean problem `a && !a` with one Bool auto param a. Single constraint And(a_ref, Not(a_ref)). Expect SolveResult::Infeasible with non-empty diagnostics.

Ensure infeasible detection works correctly. When backtracking exhausts all domain values for all variables without finding a satisfying assignment, return Infeasible with a diagnostic message listing the constraint count and param count. Should already work from step-2 but verify diagnostic quality.

Write failing test: implication `if a then b` encoded as Or(Not(a_ref), b_ref) with two Bool auto params a, b. Verify that the solution satisfies: if a is true then b must be true. Also add a test with two constraints: And(a_ref, Or(Not(a_ref), b_ref)) forcing a=true, which means b must be true.
Implication should already work from boolean backtracking + eval_expr. Verify tests pass. No new code expected.

Write failing test: cardinality 'at most 2 of [a, b, c, d] are true' with 4 Bool auto params. Encode as conjunction: Not(And(a, And(b, c))) && Not(And(a, And(b, d))) && Not(And(a, And(c, d))) && Not(And(b, And(c, d))). This says no 3 of the 4 can all be true. Expect Solved with at most 2 true values in the assignment.
Cardinality should work via boolean backtracking and eval_expr. Verify tests pass. No new code expected.

Write failing test: enum constraint. Auto param x with Type::Enum('Material'), no bounds. Constraints: (1) Ne(x_ref, enum_literal('Material','A')) and (2) Or(Eq(x_ref, enum_literal('Material','B')), Eq(x_ref, enum_literal('Material','C'))). Use CompiledExpr::literal(Value::Enum{type_name:'Material', variant:'A'}, Type::Enum('Material')) for enum literals and value_ref_typed('Part','x', Type::Enum('Material')) for param ref. Expect Solved with x = B or C.

Add enum domain support to build_variable_domains: (1) for Type::Enum auto params, scan all constraint expressions for Value::Enum literals with matching type_name using CompiledExpr::walk; (2) collect unique variants as domain values (Vec<Value::Enum>); (3) if no variants found, return NoProgress with reason. Update backtrack to handle enum values in trial map.

Write failing test: integer constraint `x + y == 10 && x >= 0 && y >= 0 && x <= 10 && y <= 10` with two Int auto params x, y each with bounds Some((0.0, 10.0)). Constraints: Eq(Add(x_ref, y_ref), literal(Int(10))), Ge(x_ref, literal(Int(0))), Ge(y_ref, literal(Int(0))), Le(x_ref, literal(Int(10))), Le(y_ref, literal(Int(10))). Use value_ref_typed with Type::Int. Expect Solved with x+y==10 and both in [0,10].

Add integer domain support to build_variable_domains: (1) for Type::Int auto params, read bounds Option<(f64, f64)> and cast to i64 range lo..=hi; (2) cap domain size at MAX_INT_DOMAIN (1000), return NoProgress if exceeded; (3) enumerate domain as Vec<Value::Int(v)> for v in lo..=hi. Update backtrack trial insertion to handle Value::Int.
Write failing test: all-different on 3 Int auto params x, y, z each with bounds [1,3]. Encode as Ne(x,y) && Ne(x,z) && Ne(y,z). Expect Solved with {x,y,z} being a permutation of {1,2,3}.
All-different should work via integer backtracking + Ne evaluation. Verify tests pass. No new code expected beyond step-12.

Write failing test: SolverRegistry integration. Create SolverRegistry::with_solvers(dimensional=Box::new(DimensionalSolver), geometric=None, logical=Some(Box::new(CpSatSolver)), fallback=None). Submit a problem with 2 Bool auto params and a logical constraint (And(a, b)). Both constraints should classify as Logical via ConstraintClassifier, routing to CpSatSolver. Expect Solved with a=true and b=true.

Ensure CpSatSolver is fully exported from lib.rs and works with SolverRegistry. The dispatch infrastructure already exists in registry.rs - this step verifies the end-to-end integration. Ensure `pub use cpsat::CpSatSolver` is in lib.rs.

Write test: CpSatSolver is Send + Sync (required by ConstraintSolver supertrait). Static assertion: fn assert_send_sync<T: Send + Sync>() {} assert_send_sync::<CpSatSolver>(). Also test empty problem (no auto params) returns Solved with empty values.

Ensure CpSatSolver is Send + Sync. As a unit struct with no interior state, this is automatically derived. Also ensure the empty-problem fast path works (no params -> Solved with empty HashMap). Verify all tests pass with `cargo test -p reify-constraints`.

Write failing tests for integer overflow in build_variable_domain (review issue: integer_overflow). Add these tests to cpsat_tests.rs:

1. `int_domain_infinity_bounds_returns_error` — Int auto param with bounds `(f64::NEG_INFINITY, f64::INFINITY)`. Must NOT panic; must return NoProgress/error with a diagnostic about non-representable bounds.

2. `int_domain_large_finite_bounds_returns_error` — Int auto param with bounds `(-1e18, 1e18)`. These cast to near i64::MIN/MAX via saturating cast; `hi_i - lo_i + 1` would overflow i64. Must NOT panic; must return NoProgress/error.

3. `int_domain_nan_bound_returns_error` — Int auto param with bounds `(f64::NAN, 10.0)`. NaN is not finite. Must NOT panic; must return NoProgress/error.

4. `int_domain_positive_infinity_upper_returns_error` — Int auto param with bounds `(0.0, f64::INFINITY)`. Only upper bound is infinite. Must NOT panic; must return NoProgress/error.

5. `int_domain_exceeds_i64_range_returns_error` — Int auto param with bounds `(-1e19, 1e19)` (values outside i64 range entirely). Must NOT panic; must return NoProgress/error.

All tests create a minimal ResolutionProblem with one Int auto param, one trivial constraint (e.g., Ge(x_ref, literal(Int(0)))), and assert that `solver.solve(...)` returns `SolveResult::NoProgress { .. }` (not panic, not Infeasible). The key assertion is that these tests do NOT panic — the current code panics in debug mode due to integer overflow at line 55.

design_decisions.decision: Pure-Rust backtracking solver instead of OR-Tools C++ FFI
design_decisions.rationale: OR-Tools FFI would require complex C++ build infrastructure (cxx or protobuf), cross-platform compilation issues, and significant maintenance burden. The task description explicitly suggests simpler alternatives for v0.1. A pure-Rust backtracking solver handles all required constraint types, has zero external dependencies, compiles fast, and is trivially testable. Problem sizes in v0.1 CAD parametric modeling are small (typically 1-10 discrete variables), making backtracking perfectly adequate.

design_decisions.decision: Use reify_expr::eval_expr as the constraint checker rather than building a constraint propagation engine
design_decisions.rationale: The existing expression evaluator already handles all CompiledExprKind variants correctly. Using it as a black-box constraint checker means: (1) automatic support for any expression type without parsing/translation, (2) guaranteed consistency with the rest of the system, (3) no maintenance burden when new expression types are added. The trade-off is no constraint propagation (only forward-checking), but this is acceptable for v0.1 problem sizes.

design_decisions.decision: Extract enum variant domains from constraint literal expressions
design_decisions.rationale: AutoParam doesn't carry enum variant information (only Type::Enum(name)). Scanning constraint expressions for Value::Enum literals with matching type_name is a pragmatic way to discover the domain without requiring changes to the ResolutionProblem struct. If no variants are found, the solver returns NoProgress, which is a safe fallback.

design_decisions.decision: Cap integer domains at a maximum size (1000 values)
design_decisions.rationale: Integer domains could be very large (e.g., bounds 0..1_000_000). Enumerating all values would be impractical. Capping at a reasonable size and returning NoProgress for larger domains prevents performance issues while covering typical v0.1 use cases. A more sophisticated integer solver (e.g., constraint propagation, arc consistency) can be added later.

design_decisions.decision: Name the struct CpSatSolver to match the task specification
design_decisions.rationale: The task explicitly names the struct CpSatSolver and describes the ConstraintSolver trait impl. Using this name maintains consistency with the task description and makes the intent clear (a solver for the same class of problems CP-SAT handles, even though the v0.1 implementation is a simple backtracking solver).

design_decisions.decision: Forward-checking during backtracking instead of naive generate-and-test
design_decisions.rationale: After assigning each variable, evaluate only constraints whose referenced auto params are all assigned. This prunes the search tree early without requiring full constraint propagation. Much more efficient than trying all complete assignments, especially for cardinality and all-different constraints where early violations eliminate large sub-trees.

design_decisions.decision: Validate f64 bounds before i64 cast with checked arithmetic for domain size
design_decisions.rationale: The f64-to-i64 saturating cast silently converts infinity/large values to i64::MAX/MIN, and the subsequent subtraction overflows. Validating is_finite() and range-checking before casting prevents silent corruption. Using checked_sub/checked_add prevents debug panics and release-mode wrapping. Returning Err (mapped to NoProgress) is the correct fallback — the solver cannot handle unrepresentable domains, so it should gracefully decline rather than panic or produce garbage.

reuse.how: Use as the constraint evaluation engine: insert trial values into ValueMap, eval constraint expression, check for Value::Bool(true). Same pattern as DimensionalSolver's build_trial_values + eval.
Implement the trait for CpSatSolver. The trait is already defined with solve(&self, problem: &ResolutionProblem) -> SolveResult.

The registry already has a `logical: Option<Box<dyn ConstraintSolver>>` field and solver_for() dispatch for ConstraintDomain::Logical. CpSatSolver plugs directly into this slot with no registry changes needed.
Already classifies bool-only constraints as ConstraintDomain::Logical, ensuring they route to the logical solver in the registry. No changes needed.

Use existing helpers: literal(), value_ref_typed(), and/or/not/eq/ne/gt/lt(), vcid(), cnid() for constructing test expressions. For enum literals, use CompiledExpr::literal() directly since the test-support literal() function panics on enum values by design.
Walk constraint expression trees to collect ValueCellId references for mapping constraints to variables. Same pattern used in decompose.rs collect_value_refs.
Reuse the pattern of cloning current_values ValueMap and inserting trial auto-param values for constraint evaluation. The ValueMap clone is O(1) via PersistentMap structural sharing.
